mn122:1.2: at one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling among the Sakyans at Kapilavatthu, in Nigrodha’s Park.
mn122:10.1: Then, Ānanda, that *bhikkhu* should settle, calm, unify, and concentrate the mind [*cittaṁ*] internally itself in that same previous *nimitta* of *samādhi*.
mn122:10.10: As he attends to the imperturbable, his mind [*cittaṁ*] springs forward, settles, stands firm, and becomes liberated in the imperturbable.
mn122:10.11: When this is so, Ānanda, the *bhikkhu* understands thus:
mn122:10.12: ‘As I attend to the imperturbable, my mind [*cittaṁ*] springs forward, settles, stands firm, and becomes liberated in the imperturbable.’
mn122:10.13: In this way he is fully aware [*sampajāno*] there.
mn122:10.3: As he attends to internal emptiness, his mind [*cittaṁ*] springs forward, settles, stands firm, and becomes liberated in internal emptiness.
mn122:10.4: When this is so, Ānanda, the *bhikkhu* understands thus:
mn122:10.5: ‘As I attend to internal emptiness, my mind [*cittaṁ*] springs forward, settles, stands firm, and becomes liberated in internal emptiness.’
mn122:10.6: In this way he is fully aware [*sampajāno*] there.
mn122:11.1: If, Ānanda, while that *bhikkhu* is dwelling in this abiding, his mind [*cittaṁ*] inclines to walking, he walks:
mn122:11.10: If, Ānanda, while that *bhikkhu* is dwelling in this abiding, his mind [*cittaṁ*] inclines to lying down, he lies down:
mn122:11.11: ‘As I am lying down in this way, evil unwholesome *dhammas* of covetousness and distress will not flow in upon me.’
mn122:11.12: In this way he is fully aware [*sampajāno*] there.
mn122:11.2: ‘As I am walking in this way, evil unwholesome *dhammas* of covetousness and distress will not flow in upon me.’
mn122:11.3: In this way he is fully aware [*sampajāno*] there.
mn122:11.4: If, Ānanda, while that *bhikkhu* is dwelling in this abiding, his mind [*cittaṁ*] inclines to standing, he stands:
mn122:11.5: ‘As I am standing in this way, evil unwholesome *dhammas* of covetousness and distress will not flow in upon me.’
mn122:11.6: In this way he is fully aware [*sampajāno*] there.
mn122:11.7: If, Ānanda, while that *bhikkhu* is dwelling in this abiding, his mind [*cittaṁ*] inclines to sitting, he sits:
mn122:11.8: ‘As I am sitting in this way, evil unwholesome *dhammas* of covetousness and distress will not flow in upon me.’
mn122:11.9: In this way he is fully aware [*sampajāno*] there.
mn122:12.1: If, Ānanda, while that *bhikkhu* is dwelling in this abiding, his mind [*cittaṁ*] inclines to speech, he thinks:
mn122:12.2: ‘That kind of talk which is low, village-like, ordinary, non-noble, unconnected with benefit, not leading to disenchantment, dispassion, cessation, stilling, direct knowledge, awakening, or *nibbāna*, namely: talk about kings, talk about thieves, talk about ministers, talk about armies, talk about dangers, talk about wars, talk about food, talk about drink, talk about clothes, talk about beds, talk about garlands, talk about perfumes, talk about relatives, talk about vehicles, talk about villages, talk about towns, talk about cities, talk about countries, talk about women, talk about liquor, talk about streets, talk about wells, talk about the departed, rambling talk, talk about the world, talk about the sea, and talk about whether things exist or do not exist, and suchlike—I will not speak such talk.’
mn122:12.3: In this way he is fully aware [*sampajāno*] there.
mn122:12.4: But that talk, Ānanda, which is truly effacing, suitable for uncovering the mind [*ceto*], and leads solely to disenchantment, dispassion, cessation, stilling, direct knowledge, awakening, and *nibbāna*, namely: talk about fewness of wishes, talk about contentment, talk about seclusion [*paviveka*], talk about non-association, talk about arousing energy, talk about ethical conduct, talk about *samādhi*, talk about discernment [*paññā*], talk about liberation, talk about the knowledge and vision of liberation—he thinks: ‘I will speak such talk.’
mn122:12.5: In this way he is fully aware [*sampajāno*] there.
mn122:13.1: If, Ānanda, while that *bhikkhu* is dwelling in this abiding, his mind [*cittaṁ*] inclines to thought [*vitakkāya*], he thinks:
mn122:13.2: ‘Those thoughts [*vitakkā*] which are low, village-like, ordinary, non-noble, unconnected with benefit, not leading to disenchantment, dispassion, cessation, stilling, direct knowledge, awakening, or *nibbāna*, namely: sensual thought [*kāmavitakko*], malicious thought [*byāpādavitakko*], and harmful thought [*vihiṁsāvitakko*]—I will not think [*vitakkessāmi*] such thoughts [*vitakke*].’
mn122:13.3: In this way he is fully aware [*sampajāno*] there.
mn122:13.4: But those thoughts [*vitakkā*], Ānanda, which are noble, leading out, and lead the one who acts accordingly to the right ending of *dukkha*, namely: thought of renunciation [*nekkhammavitakko*], thought of non-ill-will [*abyāpādavitakko*], and thought of non-harming [*avihiṁsāvitakko*]—he thinks: ‘I will think [*vitakkessāmi*] such thoughts [*vitakke*].’
mn122:13.5: In this way he is fully aware [*sampajāno*] there.
mn122:14.1: There are these five strands of sensual pleasure, Ānanda.
mn122:14.3: Forms [*rūpā*] cognizable by the eye, wished for, desired, agreeable, pleasing in form [*piyarūpā*], connected with sensual pleasure, alluring;
mn122:14.7: tangibles cognizable by the body, wished for, desired, agreeable, pleasing in form [*piyarūpā*], connected with sensual pleasure, alluring—
mn122:14.8: these, Ānanda, are the five strands of sensual pleasure.
mn122:15.1: Here a *bhikkhu* should frequently review his own mind [*cittaṁ*]:
mn122:15.10: when this is so, Ānanda, the *bhikkhu* understands thus:
mn122:15.11: ‘Whatever desire and passion there is for these five strands of sensual pleasure, that has been abandoned in me.’
mn122:15.12: In this way he is fully aware [*sampajāno*] there.
mn122:15.2: ‘Does any movement of the mind arise in me toward one or another dimension [*āyatane*] among these five strands of sensual pleasure?’
mn122:15.3: If, Ānanda, while reviewing, a *bhikkhu* understands thus:
mn122:15.4: ‘A movement of the mind does arise in me toward one or another dimension [*āyatane*] among these five strands of sensual pleasure,’
mn122:15.5: when this is so, Ānanda, the *bhikkhu* understands thus:
mn122:15.6: ‘Whatever desire and passion there is for these five strands of sensual pleasure, that has not been abandoned in me.’
mn122:15.7: In this way he is fully aware [*sampajāno*] there.
mn122:15.8: But if, Ānanda, while reviewing, a *bhikkhu* understands thus:
mn122:15.9: ‘No movement of the mind arises in me toward one or another dimension [*āyatane*] among these five strands of sensual pleasure,’
mn122:16.1: There are these five aggregates of *upādāna*, Ānanda, in regard to which a *bhikkhu* should dwell contemplating arising and passing away:
mn122:16.2: ‘Such is form [*rūpaṁ*], such is the arising of form [*rūpassa*], such is the passing away of form [*rūpassa*];
mn122:16.6: such is *viññāṇa*, such is the arising of *viññāṇa*, such is the passing away of *viññāṇa*.’
mn122:17.1: As he dwells contemplating arising and passing away in these five aggregates of *upādāna*, whatever conceit ‘I am’ there is in regard to the five aggregates of *upādāna* is abandoned.
mn122:17.2: When this is so, Ānanda, the *bhikkhu* understands thus:
mn122:17.3: ‘Whatever conceit “I am” there is in regard to these five aggregates of *upādāna*, that has been abandoned in me.’
mn122:17.4: In this way he is fully aware [*sampajāno*] there.
mn122:18.1: These, Ānanda, are *dhammas* that are wholly wholesome, having wholesomeness as their outcome, noble, supramundane, and not entered upon by the Evil One.
mn122:19.2: Seeing what purpose should a disciple follow the teacher, even when being driven away?”
mn122:19.3: “Our *dhammas* are rooted in the *Bhagavā*, *bhante*, guided by the *Bhagavā*, with the *Bhagavā* as refuge. It would be *sādhu*, *bhante*, if the meaning of this statement would occur to the *Bhagavā* himself. Having heard it from the *Bhagavā*, the *bhikkhus* will remember it.”
mn122:2.1: Then, in the morning, the *Bhagavā* dressed, took his bowl and robe, and entered Kapilavatthu for alms.
mn122:2.10: Sitting there, the *Bhagavā* addressed Venerable Ānanda:
mn122:2.11: “Many lodgings have been prepared, Ānanda, in the dwelling of Kāḷakhemaka the Sakyan.
mn122:2.13: “Many lodgings, *bhante*, have been prepared in the dwelling of Kāḷakhemaka the Sakyan.
mn122:2.15: It is robe-making time for us, *bhante*.”
mn122:2.2: Having walked for alms in Kapilavatthu, after the meal, returning from almsround, he went to the dwelling of Kāḷakhemaka the Sakyan for the day’s abiding.
mn122:2.3: Now at that time, in the dwelling of Kāḷakhemaka the Sakyan, many lodgings had been prepared.
mn122:2.4: The *Bhagavā* saw that in the dwelling of Kāḷakhemaka the Sakyan many lodgings had been prepared.
mn122:2.5: Having seen this, it occurred to the *Bhagavā*:
mn122:2.6: “Many lodgings have been prepared in the dwelling of Kāḷakhemaka the Sakyan.
mn122:2.8: Now at that time Venerable Ānanda, together with many *bhikkhus*, was doing robe-work at the dwelling of Ghaṭā the Sakyan.
mn122:2.9: Then, in the evening, the *Bhagavā* emerged from seclusion and went to the dwelling of Ghaṭā the Sakyan; having gone there, he sat down on the prepared seat.
mn122:20.1: “A disciple should not follow the teacher, Ānanda, for the sake of this, namely: *sutta*, verse, or exposition.
mn122:20.3: For a long time, Ānanda, those *dhammas* have been heard by you, retained, verbally recited, examined by the mind, and well penetrated by view.
mn122:20.4: But that talk, Ānanda, which is truly effacing, suitable for uncovering the mind, and leads solely to disenchantment, dispassion, cessation, stilling, direct knowledge, awakening, and *nibbāna*, namely: talk about fewness of wishes, talk about contentment, talk about seclusion [*paviveka*], talk about non-association, talk about arousing energy, talk about ethical conduct, talk about *samādhi*, talk about discernment [*paññā*], talk about liberation, talk about the knowledge and vision of liberation—for the sake of talk like this, Ānanda, a disciple should follow the teacher, even when being driven away.
mn122:21.1: When this is so, Ānanda, there is the teacher’s disaster; when this is so, there is the pupil’s disaster; when this is so, there is the holy-life companion’s disaster.
mn122:22.1: And how, Ānanda, is there the teacher’s disaster?
mn122:22.2: Here, Ānanda, a certain teacher resorts to a secluded lodging: a wilderness, the root of a tree, a mountain, a ravine, a mountain cave, a charnel ground, a forest thicket, the open air, or a heap of straw.
mn122:22.3: While he is dwelling withdrawn in that way, brahmins and householders, people of town and countryside, come around him.
mn122:22.4: When brahmins and householders, people of town and countryside, come around him, he takes pleasure in infatuation, falls into greed, and turns back to abundance.
mn122:22.5: This, Ānanda, is called a teacher who has met disaster.
mn122:22.6: Because of the teacher’s disaster, evil unwholesome *dhammas* have struck him down: defiling, leading to renewed *bhava*, troublesome, ripening in *dukkha*, and leading to future birth, aging, and death.
mn122:22.7: In this way, Ānanda, there is the teacher’s disaster.
mn122:23.1: And how, Ānanda, is there the pupil’s disaster?
mn122:23.2: Then, Ānanda, a disciple of that very teacher, developing the seclusion [*viveka*] of that teacher,
mn122:23.3: resorts to a secluded lodging: a wilderness, the root of a tree, a mountain, a ravine, a mountain cave, a charnel ground, a forest thicket, the open air, or a heap of straw.
mn122:23.4: While he is dwelling withdrawn in that way, brahmins and householders, people of town and countryside, come around him.
mn122:23.5: When brahmins and householders, people of town and countryside, come around him, he takes pleasure in infatuation, falls into greed, and turns back to abundance.
mn122:23.6: This, Ānanda, is called a pupil who has met disaster.
mn122:23.7: Because of the pupil’s disaster, evil unwholesome *dhammas* have struck him down: defiling, leading to renewed *bhava*, troublesome, ripening in *dukkha*, and leading to future birth, aging, and death.
mn122:23.8: In this way, Ānanda, there is the pupil’s disaster.
mn122:24.1: And how, Ānanda, is there the holy-life companion’s disaster?
mn122:24.10: This, Ānanda, is called a holy-life companion who has met disaster.
mn122:24.11: Because of the holy-life companion’s disaster, evil unwholesome *dhammas* have struck him down: defiling, leading to renewed *bhava*, troublesome, ripening in *dukkha*, and leading to future birth, aging, and death.
mn122:24.12: In this way, Ānanda, there is the holy-life companion’s disaster.
mn122:24.13: In this regard, Ānanda, this teacher’s disaster and this pupil’s disaster are not as painful in result or as bitter in result as the holy-life companion’s disaster, and moreover it leads to downfall.
mn122:24.2: Here, Ānanda, a Tathāgata arises in the world, an *arahant*, perfectly awakened, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, fortunate, knower of the world, unsurpassed trainer of persons to be tamed, teacher of *devas* and humans, awakened, the *Bhagavā*.
mn122:24.3: He resorts to a secluded lodging: a wilderness, the root of a tree, a mountain, a ravine, a mountain cave, a charnel ground, a forest thicket, the open air, or a heap of straw.
mn122:24.4: While he is dwelling withdrawn in that way, brahmins and householders, people of town and countryside, come around him.
mn122:24.5: When brahmins and householders, people of town and countryside, come around him, he does not take pleasure in infatuation, does not fall into greed, and does not turn back to abundance.
mn122:24.6: Then, Ānanda, a disciple of that very teacher, developing the seclusion [*viveka*] of that teacher,
mn122:24.7: resorts to a secluded lodging: a wilderness, the root of a tree, a mountain, a ravine, a mountain cave, a charnel ground, a forest thicket, the open air, or a heap of straw.
mn122:24.8: While he is dwelling withdrawn in that way, brahmins and householders, people of town and countryside, come around him.
mn122:24.9: When brahmins and householders, people of town and countryside, come around him, he takes pleasure in infatuation, falls into greed, and turns back to abundance.
mn122:25.1: Therefore, Ānanda, conduct yourselves toward me with friendliness, not with hostility.
mn122:25.2: That will be for your welfare and *sukha* for a long time.
mn122:25.3: And how, Ānanda, do disciples conduct themselves toward the teacher with hostility, not with friendliness?
mn122:25.4: Here, Ānanda, the teacher teaches the *dhamma* to the disciples, compassionate, seeking their welfare, out of compassion:
mn122:25.5: ‘This is for your welfare; this is for your *sukha*.’
mn122:25.6: His disciples do not wish to listen, do not lend ear, do not set their mind [*cittaṁ*] on understanding, and they turn aside from the teacher’s instruction.
mn122:25.7: In this way, Ānanda, disciples conduct themselves toward the teacher with hostility, not with friendliness.
mn122:26.1: And how, Ānanda, do disciples conduct themselves toward the teacher with friendliness, not with hostility?
mn122:26.2: Here, Ānanda, the teacher teaches the *dhamma* to the disciples, compassionate, seeking their welfare, out of compassion:
mn122:26.3: ‘This is for your welfare; this is for your *sukha*.’
mn122:26.4: His disciples wish to listen, lend ear, set their mind [*cittaṁ*] on understanding, and they do not turn aside from the teacher’s instruction.
mn122:26.5: In this way, Ānanda, disciples conduct themselves toward the teacher with friendliness, not with hostility.
mn122:26.6: Therefore, Ānanda, conduct yourselves toward me with friendliness, not with hostility.
mn122:26.7: That will be for your welfare and *sukha* for a long time.
mn122:27.1: I will not deal with you, Ānanda, as a potter deals with raw, unfired vessels.
mn122:27.2: Again and again restraining, Ānanda, I will speak;
mn122:27.3: again and again pressing, Ānanda, I will speak.
mn122:27.6: Satisfied, Venerable Ānanda rejoiced in the *Bhagavā*’s words.
mn122:27.7: The Greater Discourse on Emptiness is finished, the second.
mn122:3.1: “A *bhikkhu* does not shine, Ānanda, when he delights in company, rejoices in company, is devoted to delighting in company, delights in a group, rejoices in a group, and takes joy in a group.
mn122:3.2: That a *bhikkhu*, Ānanda, who delights in company, rejoices in company, is devoted to delighting in company, delights in a group, rejoices in a group, and takes joy in a group, will become one who gains at will, without trouble, without difficulty, that *sukha* of renunciation, the *sukha* of seclusion, the *sukha* of stilling, the *sukha* of awakening—this is not possible.
mn122:3.3: But a *bhikkhu*, Ānanda, who lives alone, withdrawn from the group: it can be expected of that *bhikkhu* that he will become one who gains at will, without trouble, without difficulty, that *sukha* of renunciation, the *sukha* of seclusion, the *sukha* of stilling, the *sukha* of awakening—this is possible.
mn122:4.1: That a *bhikkhu*, Ānanda, who delights in company, rejoices in company, is devoted to delighting in company, delights in a group, rejoices in a group, and takes joy in a group, will enter and dwell in a lovely liberation of mind, whether temporary or non-temporary and unshakable—this is not possible.
mn122:4.2: But a *bhikkhu*, Ānanda, who lives alone, withdrawn from the group: it can be expected of that *bhikkhu* that he will enter and dwell in a lovely liberation of mind, whether temporary or non-temporary and unshakable—this is possible.
mn122:5.1: I do not see even one form [*rūpampi*], Ānanda, in delighting in which, taking pleasure in which, from the alteration and change of that form [*rūpassa*], sorrow, lamentation, *dukkha*, distress, and despair would not arise.
mn122:6.1: But this abiding, Ānanda, has been fully awakened to by the Tathāgata, namely—
mn122:6.2: by non-attention to all *nimittas*, to enter and dwell in internal emptiness.
mn122:6.3: If, Ānanda, while the Tathāgata is dwelling in this abiding, *bhikkhus*, *bhikkhunis*, laymen, laywomen, kings, royal ministers, wanderers of other sects, and disciples of wanderers of other sects approach him,
mn122:6.4: then, Ānanda, with his mind [*cittena*] sloping to seclusion [*viveka*], inclining to seclusion [*viveka*], leaning to seclusion [*viveka*], withdrawn, delighting in renunciation, entirely finished with all *dhammas* that are bases for *āsavas*, the Tathāgata speaks only talk connected with dismissal.
mn122:7.1: Therefore, Ānanda, if a *bhikkhu* should wish:
mn122:7.2: ‘May I enter and dwell in internal emptiness,’ then, Ānanda, that *bhikkhu* should settle, calm, unify [*ekodi*], and concentrate the mind [*cittaṁ*] internally itself.
mn122:7.3: And how, Ānanda, does a *bhikkhu* settle, calm, unify, and concentrate the mind [*cittaṁ*] internally itself?
mn122:8.1: Here, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu*, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unwholesome *dhammas* … enters and dwells in the first *jhāna* …
mn122:8.4: he enters and dwells in the fourth *jhāna*.
mn122:8.5: In this way, Ānanda, a *bhikkhu* settles, calms, unifies, and concentrates the mind [*cittaṁ*] internally itself.
mn122:9.10: When this is so, Ānanda, the *bhikkhu* understands thus:
mn122:9.11: ‘As I attend to the imperturbable, my mind [*cittaṁ*] does not spring forward, settle, stand firm, or become liberated in the imperturbable.’
mn122:9.12: In this way he is fully aware [*sampajāno*] there.
mn122:9.2: As he attends to internal emptiness, his mind [*cittaṁ*] does not spring forward, settle, stand firm, or become liberated in internal emptiness.
mn122:9.3: When this is so, Ānanda, the *bhikkhu* understands thus:
mn122:9.4: ‘As I attend to internal emptiness, my mind [*cittaṁ*] does not spring forward, settle, stand firm, or become liberated in internal emptiness.’
mn122:9.5: In this way he is fully aware [*sampajāno*] there.
mn122:9.9: As he attends to the imperturbable, his mind [*cittaṁ*] does not spring forward, settle, stand firm, or become liberated in the imperturbable.
